questions.stem: A patient on nasal cannula oxygen becomes more tachypneic and says, "I can't catch my breath." What is the most important nursing interpretation?

options.text: Respiratory status may be worsening despite oxygen delivery
Nasal cannula use guarantees adequate oxygenation
Tachypnea is always expected with oxygen therapy
The complaint is only important if the patient also has fever

questions.rationale: Supplemental oxygen does not prevent deterioration. Increasing distress while on oxygen should prompt immediate reassessment.

distractorRationales.b: Nasal cannula use guarantees adequate oxygenation misses the clinical pattern: Oxygen Device Reassessment oxygen points to Respiratory status may be worsening despite oxygen delivery, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.c: Tachypnea is always expected with oxygen therapy misses the clinical pattern: Oxygen Device Reassessment oxygen points to Respiratory status may be worsening despite oxygen delivery, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.d: The complaint is only important if the patient also has fever misses the clinical pattern: Oxygen Device Reassessment oxygen points to Respiratory status may be worsening despite oxygen delivery, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.

questions.takeaway: Oxygen in place does not mean the patient is stable.

visualRationale.title: oxygen_device_reassessment
visualRationale.caption: Oxygen in place does not mean the patient is stable.
visualRationale.conclusion: Oxygen in place does not mean the patient is stable.

questions.stem: A patient with heart failure has cool skin, weak peripheral pulses, and low urine output. What is the best interpretation?

options.text: Low forward flow may be limiting perfusion
The patient is stable because blood pressure is measurable
Weak pulses are not clinically useful
Urine output matters only in kidney disease

questions.rationale: Cool skin, weak pulses, and oliguria suggest reduced cardiac output and poor perfusion.

distractorRationales.b: The patient is stable because blood pressure is measurable misses the clinical pattern: Heart Failure Perfusion Clue perfusion points to Low forward flow may be limiting perfusion, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen circulation or perfusion.
distractorRationales.c: Weak pulses are not clinically useful misses the clinical pattern: Heart Failure Perfusion Clue perfusion points to Low forward flow may be limiting perfusion, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen circulation or perfusion.
distractorRationales.d: Urine output matters only in kidney disease misses the clinical pattern: Heart Failure Perfusion Clue perfusion points to Low forward flow may be limiting perfusion, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen circulation or perfusion.

questions.takeaway: Low-output states often show up in peripheral perfusion findings.

visualRationale.title: heart_failure_perfusion_clue
visualRationale.caption: Low-output states often show up in peripheral perfusion findings.
visualRationale.conclusion: Low-output states often show up in peripheral perfusion findings.

questions.stem: A patient suddenly develops facial droop and slurred speech. What should the nurse do first?

options.text: Activate urgent stroke-focused assessment and response
Reassess in one hour
Ask the patient to walk to the bathroom first
Offer water and observe

questions.rationale: Acute focal neurologic deficits suggest possible stroke and require immediate assessment and escalation.

distractorRationales.b: Reassess in one hour misses the clinical pattern: Acute Stroke Warning stroke points to Activate urgent stroke-focused assessment and response, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen neurologic deterioration.
distractorRationales.c: Ask the patient to walk to the bathroom first misses the clinical pattern: Acute Stroke Warning stroke points to Activate urgent stroke-focused assessment and response, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen neurologic deterioration.
distractorRationales.d: Offer water and observe misses the clinical pattern: Acute Stroke Warning stroke points to Activate urgent stroke-focused assessment and response, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen neurologic deterioration.

questions.takeaway: New focal neurologic change is time-sensitive.

questions.stem: A diabetic patient becomes shaky, pale, and confused, and the glucose is 48 mg/dL. What is the priority?

options.text: Treat the hypoglycemia promptly
Delay treatment until the next meal tray arrives
Recheck the glucose in two hours
Encourage the patient to sleep

questions.rationale: Symptomatic hypoglycemia requires prompt treatment to prevent worsening neurologic compromise.

distractorRationales.b: Delay treatment until the next meal tray arrives misses the clinical pattern: Hypoglycemia First Step hypoglycemia points to Treat the hypoglycemia promptly, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen unstable glucose physiology.
distractorRationales.c: Recheck the glucose in two hours misses the clinical pattern: Hypoglycemia First Step hypoglycemia points to Treat the hypoglycemia promptly, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen unstable glucose physiology.
distractorRationales.d: Encourage the patient to sleep misses the clinical pattern: Hypoglycemia First Step hypoglycemia points to Treat the hypoglycemia promptly, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen unstable glucose physiology.

questions.takeaway: Low glucose with symptoms is an immediate treatment problem.

questions.stem: A patient has dry mucous membranes, poor skin turgor, tachycardia, and low urine output. Which interpretation is most useful?

options.text: The patient may be volume depleted
The patient is fluid overloaded
Low urine output is unrelated to hydration
Tachycardia is expected and not useful

questions.rationale: These signs are consistent with dehydration and possible reduced circulating volume.

distractorRationales.b: The patient is fluid overloaded misses the clinical pattern: Dehydration Clue dehydration points to The patient may be volume depleted, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen clinical safety risk.
distractorRationales.c: Low urine output is unrelated to hydration misses the clinical pattern: Dehydration Clue dehydration points to The patient may be volume depleted, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen clinical safety risk.
distractorRationales.d: Tachycardia is expected and not useful misses the clinical pattern: Dehydration Clue dehydration points to The patient may be volume depleted, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen clinical safety risk.

questions.takeaway: Low urine output plus dry assessment findings suggests volume depletion.

questions.stem: A patient receiving anticoagulant therapy reports black tarry stools. What is the priority nursing interpretation?

options.text: Possible bleeding requires prompt follow-up
This is expected with all anticoagulants
Stools are unrelated to medication safety
Reassess next week if the patient is still concerned

questions.rationale: Black tarry stools can indicate GI bleeding, which is a serious safety concern in a patient on anticoagulants.

distractorRationales.b: This is expected with all anticoagulants misses the clinical pattern: Anticoagulant Safety bleeding points to Possible bleeding requires prompt follow-up, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen bleeding or anticoagulation injury.
distractorRationales.c: Stools are unrelated to medication safety misses the clinical pattern: Anticoagulant Safety bleeding points to Possible bleeding requires prompt follow-up, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen bleeding or anticoagulation injury.
distractorRationales.d: Reassess next week if the patient is still concerned misses the clinical pattern: Anticoagulant Safety bleeding points to Possible bleeding requires prompt follow-up, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen bleeding or anticoagulation injury.

questions.takeaway: Bleeding clues matter immediately during anticoagulant therapy.

questions.stem: Which action best helps reduce transmission when caring for a patient with a contagious infection?

options.text: Perform appropriate hand hygiene before and after care
Reuse gloves if they still look clean
Skip precautions if the patient feels better
Share equipment without cleaning it

questions.rationale: Hand hygiene remains one of the most effective infection prevention measures.

distractorRationales.b: Reuse gloves if they still look clean misses the clinical pattern: Infection Prevention infection points to Perform appropriate hand hygiene before and after care, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen infection progression.
distractorRationales.c: Skip precautions if the patient feels better misses the clinical pattern: Infection Prevention infection points to Perform appropriate hand hygiene before and after care, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen infection progression.
distractorRationales.d: Share equipment without cleaning it misses the clinical pattern: Infection Prevention infection points to Perform appropriate hand hygiene before and after care, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen infection progression.

questions.takeaway: Simple infection-control steps still matter most.

questions.stem: A postpartum patient has heavy vaginal bleeding, tachycardia, and dizziness. What should the nurse recognize first?

options.text: This may indicate postpartum hemorrhage
This is always expected after delivery
Dizziness is unrelated to bleeding
The patient should just rest longer

questions.rationale: Heavy bleeding with tachycardia and dizziness after delivery is concerning for hemorrhage and requires prompt attention.

distractorRationales.b: This is always expected after delivery misses the clinical pattern: Postpartum Hemorrhage Clue bleeding points to This may indicate postpartum hemorrhage, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen bleeding or anticoagulation injury.
distractorRationales.c: Dizziness is unrelated to bleeding misses the clinical pattern: Postpartum Hemorrhage Clue bleeding points to This may indicate postpartum hemorrhage, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen bleeding or anticoagulation injury.
distractorRationales.d: The patient should just rest longer misses the clinical pattern: Postpartum Hemorrhage Clue bleeding points to This may indicate postpartum hemorrhage, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen bleeding or anticoagulation injury.

questions.takeaway: Postpartum heavy bleeding plus instability is an emergency clue.

questions.stem: A child with vomiting has dry lips, no tears when crying, and decreased urine output. What is the best interpretation?

options.text: The child may be dehydrated
These findings are normal in all children with vomiting
Tears are not useful in assessment
Urine output does not help in pediatrics

questions.rationale: Dry mucous membranes, lack of tears, and decreased urine output are classic dehydration findings in children.

distractorRationales.b: These findings are normal in all children with vomiting misses the clinical pattern: Dehydration In A Child dehydration points to The child may be dehydrated, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen pediatric or newborn instability.
distractorRationales.c: Tears are not useful in assessment misses the clinical pattern: Dehydration In A Child dehydration points to The child may be dehydrated, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen pediatric or newborn instability.
distractorRationales.d: Urine output does not help in pediatrics misses the clinical pattern: Dehydration In A Child dehydration points to The child may be dehydrated, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen pediatric or newborn instability.

questions.takeaway: Pediatric dehydration often shows up in tears, urine, and mucous membranes.

questions.stem: A patient says, "I'm scared I won't get better." Which nurse response is most therapeutic?

options.text: "Tell me more about what feels most scary right now."
"You should not feel that way."
"At least you are in the hospital."
"Let's not talk about that."

questions.rationale: Open-ended exploration supports therapeutic communication by inviting the patient to share concerns without dismissing emotion.

distractorRationales.b: "You should not feel that way." misses the clinical pattern: Therapeutic Response safety points to "Tell me more about what feels most scary right now.", so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen clinical safety risk.
distractorRationales.c: "At least you are in the hospital." misses the clinical pattern: Therapeutic Response safety points to "Tell me more about what feels most scary right now.", so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen clinical safety risk.
distractorRationales.d: "Let's not talk about that." misses the clinical pattern: Therapeutic Response safety points to "Tell me more about what feels most scary right now.", so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen clinical safety risk.

questions.takeaway: Therapeutic communication validates emotion and invites more sharing.
